headline: US and Iran reignite their war over the Strait of Hormuz: Trump drops a 20% shipping fee but presses a naval blockade as strikes fly, pushing oil to a one-month high.
the_big_picture: The single thread running through today is the re-escalation of the US-Iran conflict at the Strait of Hormuz, the chokepoint that carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil. Trump backed off a threatened 20% transit fee but is pushing ahead with a naval blockade while both sides trade strikes and tankers come under attack, sending crude to a one-month high. That oil spike is the hinge for everything downstream: US inflation cooled to 3.5% but officials flagged fresh upside risk, India's inflation just breached its central bank's target, the rupee slid past 96 and the Sensex fell. Running underneath, an AI storyline cuts both ways: IBM shed roughly $70bn in a day after stumbling on AI deals, even as China's AI-driven exports jumped 27% and Nvidia began shipping powerful H200 chips into China.

stories.title: A federal judge extends the freeze on Paramount's $111 billion Warner Bros. Discovery takeover to at least August 17
key_points: Judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín (Oakland, California) extended her restraining order on the merger by another 14 days
The original order — issued Monday, July 20 — would have expired Aug 3; it now runs to at least Aug 17
12 state attorneys general say the deal breaks US antitrust law by cutting competition in cable and theatrical film distribution
The Writers Guild of America has a separate suit warning the merger will suppress writers' pay; a preliminary injunction hearing is set for Aug 3
stories.background: Paramount Skydance beat out a rival Netflix bid earlier in 2026 to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery for around $111 billion, a deal that would merge two of Hollywood's biggest studios and their cable/streaming networks. Big mergers like this routinely draw antitrust scrutiny — US regulators and states can sue to block a deal even after both companies' boards have agreed to it.
stories.what_happened: The judge granted the extension because she needs more time to weigh the states' and the WGA's request for a full preliminary injunction, which could block the deal outright. Her ruling keeps Paramount and Warner Bros. Discovery from closing or integrating operations in the meantime.
stories.why_it_matters: This is one of the largest media mergers ever attempted, and a court — not just regulators — is now actively slowing it down on competition grounds. That affects thousands of jobs, what shows up on TV and in theaters, and how much choice viewers have.
stories.ripple_effects: The delay keeps both companies' stocks in limbo and could embolden other states or unions to challenge future media mega-mergers in court. If the deal ultimately collapses, Netflix — the bidder Warner Bros. Discovery turned down — could end up circling back.
stories.why_now: Courts have grown more willing to intervene in mega-mergers on antitrust grounds before regulators finish their own review; today's extension buys time for the Aug 3 preliminary injunction hearing, the next real test of whether the deal survives.
stories.watch_next: The August 3 preliminary injunction hearing — if granted, it could block the merger for months or kill it entirely.

key_terms.definition: A short-term court order that freezes an action (here, closing the merger) while a judge decides whether a longer block is warranted.
A longer-lasting court order stopping an action until a full trial can happen — the next, bigger step up from a temporary restraining order.
Laws designed to stop companies from merging or acting in ways that reduce competition and hurt consumers.

stories.the_lesson: Even after a merger is signed and priced, courts can pause it while regulators or states investigate — a deal's size and market power, not just shareholder approval, often decide whether it's allowed to close.
stories.market_link: Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery shares move on every twist in this case; a blocked deal would likely lift Netflix, the rival bidder WBD had turned down.

stories.title: The EU agrees its 21st Russia sanctions package targeting banks — after Greece wins a year-long exemption to keep shipping Russian gas
key_points: EU ambassadors agreed a 21st sanctions package against Russia over the Ukraine war
New curbs hit Russia's banking sector
Greece won a one-year, auto-renewing exemption letting EU firms keep shipping Russian LNG to other countries
The deal followed 3+ days of failed talks that forced a temporary freeze of the EU's oil price cap
stories.background: The EU has piled sanctions on Russia in rounds ever since the 2022 invasion of Ukraine — this is the 21st such package. Every round needs all 27 EU member states to agree unanimously, which is why a single country's objection can hold the whole package hostage. Greece objected this time because Greek shipowners control a large share of Europe's specialized LNG (liquefied natural gas) tanker fleet, and a full ban on carrying Russian gas would have cost them business.
stories.what_happened: EU envoys signed off on the 21st sanctions package on July 23, adding new restrictions on Russian banks. To get Greece on board, the deal carves out a one-year exemption (renewing automatically) letting European companies keep shipping Russian LNG to non-EU countries, but only under contracts signed before Russia's February 2022 invasion.
stories.why_it_matters: It shows the EU can still tighten the financial screws on Russia's war effort despite internal splits, but the Greek carve-out is a reminder that these sanctions packages are rarely airtight — they're a political compromise, not a clean embargo.
stories.ripple_effects: Russian banks get further cut off from EU finance, squeezing how Russia funds its war. Greek shipping firms are protected from losing LNG-carrying business. Watch for Moscow's response (it has previously retaliated with counter-sanctions) and whether other EU states now push for their own carve-outs in future rounds.
stories.why_now: This is the year's 21st sanctions round, agreed right after a summer standoff over the EU's price cap on Russian oil, which had to be temporarily frozen while the Greece dispute was resolved.
stories.watch_next: Formal sign-off by all 27 EU governments and the package's actual entry into force — plus any retaliatory move from Russia.

key_terms.definition: A bundled set of economic penalties — like asset freezes or trade bans — that a government or bloc imposes together, usually named by number as more rounds get added over time.
The EU requirement that all 27 member states must agree before new sanctions take effect, giving any single country an effective veto.
Liquefied natural gas — natural gas cooled into liquid form so it can be shipped by tanker instead of pipeline.

stories.the_lesson: Sanctions regimes need unanimous political buy-in from every country involved, so they often end up watered down with carve-outs for whichever country has the most to lose — meaning 'tough' sanctions rarely apply as uniformly as the headlines suggest.
stories.market_link: Watch European shipping and energy stocks, the Russian ruble, and Brent crude for reaction to tighter sanctions on Russian banks.

stories.title: Russia fires over 100 drones and missiles at Ukraine overnight, killing two people in a Donetsk apartment strike
key_points: Russia launched 100+ strike drones (mostly Iranian-designed Shaheds) and 4+ missiles overnight into July 24
A strike on Kostyantynivka, in Ukraine's Donetsk region, killed 2 people and injured 14
Five apartment buildings, 8 houses, shops and vehicles were damaged
The attack came as fighting continued along the front line with no ceasefire in place
stories.background: Kostyantynivka is a front-line town in Donetsk that Russian forces have been grinding toward for months — Russian President Vladimir Putin has even falsely claimed at times to have already captured it, which independent analysts say overstates Russia's actual, much slower progress. Overnight mass-drone-and-missile barrages like this one have become Russia's routine method of pressuring Ukrainian cities and infrastructure while ground fighting continues in the east.
stories.what_happened: Russia launched more than 100 drones and several missiles at Ukraine overnight, with President Volodymyr Zelenskyy saying most were Iranian-made Shahed drones. A strike hit residential and commercial buildings in Kostyantynivka, killing two people and injuring 14, and damaging five apartment blocks, eight houses, shops and vehicles.
stories.why_it_matters: The attack shows that, whatever diplomatic talk exists about ending the war, the fighting and civilian casualties continue on a near-daily basis. Kostyantynivka's continued resistance — despite Russian claims otherwise — is also a real-time test of how much territory Russia is actually gaining versus what it says it's gaining.
stories.ripple_effects: Continued strikes keep up pressure on Ukraine's allies to supply more air-defense systems, sustain elevated insurance costs for Black Sea grain shipping, and keep a floor under European defense-sector spending and stocks as the war shows no sign of a near-term end.
stories.why_now: The strike lands amid a stalled peace process — earlier 2026 talks in the UAE and Switzerland made no breakthrough on the core sticking point (Russia's demand that Ukraine cede territory) — while Russia keeps trying to force gains on the ground before any deal is reached.
stories.watch_next: Whether renewed peace talks (reportedly possible in two to three months per Ukrainian officials) make progress, and whether Russia's push toward Kostyantynivka continues or stalls.

key_terms.definition: a cheap, Iranian-designed exploding drone that Russia mass-produces and launches in large swarms to overwhelm Ukraine's air defenses.

stories.the_lesson: In a grinding war, a stalled 'peace process' and daily violence run in parallel — talks about a deal don't pause the fighting until an actual agreement is signed and enforced.
stories.market_link: Sustained war risk keeps European defense stocks supported and keeps Black Sea shipping-insurance premiums elevated, both of which factor into global grain and fertilizer prices.

stories.title: A wildfire near Bordeaux forces 20,000 people to flee as France's third heatwave of the summer turns the land tinder-dry
key_points: Fire near Lège-Cap Ferret, on the Atlantic coast by Bordeaux, has burned over 3,100 hectares (12 sq miles)
More than 20,000 people evacuated from campsites and coastal towns overnight
700 firefighters deployed, including reinforcements sent from the Paris region
It's France's third heatwave since May, each one drying out vegetation further
stories.background: Europe has been hit by back-to-back heatwaves in 2026, and France's Atlantic coast — pine forests packed with summer tourists — has become one of the continent's most reliable wildfire hotspots when conditions turn hot and dry. Scientists have linked the rising frequency and intensity of these heatwaves to climate change, which is turning what used to be rare, one-off events into an almost routine summer pattern.
stories.what_happened: A wildfire broke out near the tourist town of Lège-Cap Ferret, southwest of Bordeaux, and spread rapidly overnight into Thursday, burning more than 3,100 hectares. Authorities evacuated over 20,000 people — a mix of residents, campers and holidaymakers — from the area as a precaution. No injuries have been reported so far, and roughly 700 firefighters, including units brought in from around Paris, are working to contain the blaze.
stories.why_it_matters: This is the third major heatwave to hit France since May, and each one leaves the ground drier than the last — meaning the wildfire risk doesn't reset between events, it compounds. A fire this size threatens homes, a major tourist economy, and — given the region's proximity to vineyards — potentially the Bordeaux wine industry too.
stories.ripple_effects: Insurers face a growing bill for European wildfire and heat damage this year; France's tourism sector, already squeezed by repeat evacuations, takes another hit mid-season; and the fire adds to political pressure on the French government over wildfire-response funding and climate adaptation spending, an argument playing out across southern Europe as Italy and Spain battle their own blazes this week.
stories.why_now: Hot, dry conditions from France's third heatwave since May left forests and scrubland primed to burn, and strong coastal winds helped the fire spread fast once it started.
stories.watch_next: Whether firefighters can fully contain the blaze before the weekend, and whether forecast heat lets it spread toward Bordeaux's wine-growing areas.

key_terms.definition: a unit of area equal to about 2.5 acres, or roughly the size of a soccer pitch and a half — used worldwide to measure wildfire damage.
the science of estimating how much more likely or severe an extreme weather event was made by human-caused climate change.

stories.the_lesson: Heatwaves don't reset the risk clock between events — each one leaves vegetation drier than before, so a region can go from 'unlucky once' to 'a fire every few weeks' as a summer of repeat heat piles up.
stories.market_link: European insurers and reinsurers (e.g., AXA, Swiss Re) face rising wildfire claims this season, and French regional tourism and hospitality revenue takes a hit each time evacuations are ordered.

stories.title: SpaceX tries again to launch Starship on its 13th test flight — this time carrying real satellites for the first time
key_points: SpaceX is targeting Friday (today) for Starship's 13th test flight after a launch-pad abort last week
For the first time, Starship will try to deploy real payloads: 20 production Starlink V3 satellites
Engineers replaced several engines and reworked the propulsion system after the abort
Weather, not hardware, is now the main risk to today's launch window
stories.background: Starship is SpaceX's giant, fully reusable next-generation rocket, central to both its Mars ambitions and NASA's Artemis Moon-lander plans (which we covered separately). The program has flown a dozen prior test flights with mixed results, including explosions. On July 16, this 13th test flight was aborted less than a second before liftoff when several engines failed to ignite.
stories.what_happened: SpaceX says it has fixed the issue behind last week's aborted launch, replacing several engines and updating the rocket's propulsion system. After slipping from July 23, the company is now targeting a liftoff around 6:45 p.m. ET today from Starbase, Texas. The flight's headline goal is to deploy 20 production Starlink V3 satellites — SpaceX's newest, higher-capacity internet satellites — the first time Starship has released real payloads rather than dummy simulators.
stories.why_it_matters: Successfully deploying real satellites would turn Starship from a rocket that merely survives test flights into one that can actually do commercial work — launching the next generation of Starlink. It's also a confidence signal for NASA, which is separately testing whether Starship's Moon-lander variant is ready for astronauts.
stories.ripple_effects: A clean deployment would let SpaceX accelerate its Starlink V3 rollout, expanding internet bandwidth and coverage. Another failure — this program's fourth-plus setback — would renew scrutiny of Starship's reliability just as NASA weighs it against Blue Origin's competing lander.
stories.why_now: This is SpaceX's second attempt this week after last week's launchpad abort, as the company pushes to keep pace with its own aggressive test-flight schedule.
stories.watch_next: Whether the roughly 6:45 p.m. ET liftoff happens on schedule despite forecast rain and thunderstorms, and whether all 20 Starlink V3 satellites deploy successfully.

key_terms.definition: The newest, larger generation of SpaceX's Starlink internet satellites, built to carry far more bandwidth per satellite than earlier versions.

stories.the_lesson: Test programs measure progress not just by whether a rocket flies, but by what it's asked to prove each time — moving from dummy cargo to real satellites is often the true milestone, even when the flight itself looks routine from the outside.

stories.title: WHO warns Congo's Ebola outbreak is now spreading faster than contact-tracers can follow — as unpaid health workers strike
key_points: Confirmed Ebola cases have climbed to about 2,470, with the death toll above 1,030
WHO says 80% of new cases can't be traced back to a known chain of infection
Some front-line health workers have gone on strike over unpaid wages
Attacks on health facilities are also forcing response teams out of some areas
stories.background: This outbreak, declared on 15 May 2026 in eastern DR Congo, is caused by the rare Bundibugyo strain of Ebola, which has no approved vaccine or drug — unlike the more common strain most Ebola tools were built for. We reported two days ago that it had already crossed 1,000 deaths faster than any Ebola outbreak on record.
stories.what_happened: The World Health Organization and DR Congo's Ministry of Health said confirmed cases have risen to roughly 2,470, with more than 1,030 deaths. Crucially, WHO officials said about 80% of newly confirmed cases cannot be linked back to a known chain of transmission — meaning responders don't know where most new infections are coming from. Separately, some health workers involved in the response have gone on strike, saying they haven't been paid since the outbreak began, and attacks on health facilities have forced aid workers out of parts of the affected region.
stories.why_it_matters: Contact tracing — finding and monitoring everyone an infected person has been near — is the main tool used to contain an outbreak before it spreads further. When most new cases can't be traced, it's a sign the outbreak has outgrown the response, not just that it's getting bigger. Combined with striking health workers and attacks on clinics, the response itself is now struggling to function.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect pressure on WHO and international donors to rush additional emergency funding and responders. The pay dispute could deepen if not resolved quickly, further slowing containment. Untraceable spread also raises the risk of the outbreak reaching new provinces or crossing further into neighboring countries beyond the case already confirmed in Uganda.
stories.why_now: The death toll crossed 1,000 only days ago; this new WHO warning about untraceable spread and the health-worker strikes marks a more alarming turn than the milestone alone — it signals the response, not just the virus, is faltering.
stories.watch_next: Whether WHO announces emergency funding or additional international responders, and whether the health-worker pay dispute is resolved before more clinics go dark.

key_terms.definition: Identifying and monitoring everyone who has been in contact with an infected person — the main tool used to contain an outbreak before it spreads further.

stories.the_lesson: An epidemic doesn't need to accelerate in raw case numbers to become more dangerous — losing the ability to trace where new cases are coming from is often the bigger warning sign, because it means the outbreak has outgrown the tools used to contain it.

thread.previously: We reported the outbreak had crossed 1,000 deaths in under 10 weeks — the fastest-growing Ebola epidemic on record, with no approved vaccine for the Bundibugyo strain involved.
thread.changed: WHO now says confirmed cases have climbed to about 2,470, and 80% of new infections can't be traced to a known source — a sign the outbreak is outrunning containment efforts. Some health workers have also gone on strike over unpaid wages, and attacks on clinics are forcing responders out of some areas.
